question: Did the review adhere to pre-defined objectives and eligibility criteria?
answers: nan

question: Were the eligibility crieria appropriate for the review question?
answers:
Here, we provide the first extensive and comprehensive
evaluation of the prevalence of AIDs in patients with CSU and vice
versa.
In studies identified, we did a search for the prevalence of autoantibodies
(antinuclear, rheumatoid factor, anti-dsDNA, anti-parietal cell, antitransglutaminase,
anti-smooth muscle, anti-mitochondrial, anti- SSA, anti-SSB, anti-Sm, anti-cardiolipin) and a family history of
AIDs. The term "urticarial rash" was used for studies where CSU
was not clearly differentiated from acute urticaria, chronic inducible
urticaria and/or urticarial vasculitis.

question: Were all restrictions in eligibility crieria based on study characteristics appropriate (e.g. date, sample size, study quality, outcomes measured)? If yes, indicate which study characteristic was an inclusion/exclusion criteria
answers:
A Pubmed searchwas performed to identify studies published before
April 2017 with the keywords "urticaria" and "chronic urticaria"
in combination with the terms that are listed in Table 1. No language
filter was applied.

In studies identified, we did a search for the prevalence of autoantibodies (antinuclear, rheumatoid factor, anti-dsDNA, anti-parietal cell, antitransglutaminase, anti-smooth muscle, anti-mitochondrial, anti-SSA, anti-SSB, anti-Sm, anti-cardiolipin) and a family history of AIDs. The term "urticarial rash" was used for studies where CSU was not clearly differentiated from acute urticaria, chronic inducible urticaria and/or urticarial vasculitis.

answers: nan

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in data collection?
answers:
A Pubmed searchwas performed to identify studies published before
April 2017 with the keywords "urticaria" and "chronic urticaria"
in combination with the terms that are listed in Table 1. No language
filter was applied. We also examined the reference lists of the retrieved
articles and we searched Google Scholar with the same keywords
to identify additional studies. In total, 64 studies were
included in this systematic review. Most of these studies looked at
whether or not the patients included had AID at the time they were
assessed (point prevalence). A few studies reported whether patients
had a history of AID or not. In some studies, itwas not specified
if the patients still had the AID at the time they had their urticaria.
Some studies evaluated the prevalence ofmore than one AID. The selection
process of relevant studies is summarized in Table 1. In studies
identified, we did a search for the prevalence of autoantibodies
(antinuclear, rheumatoid factor, anti-dsDNA, anti-parietal cell, antitransglutaminase,
anti-smooth muscle, anti-mitochondrial, anti-
SSA, anti-SSB, anti-Sm, anti-cardiolipin) and a family history of
AIDs. The term "urticarial rash" was used for studies where CSU
was not clearly differentiated from acute urticaria, chronic inducible
urticaria and/or urticarial vasculitis.

in combination with the terms that are listed in Table 1. No language

included in this systematic review. Most of these studies looked at
whether or not the patients included had AID at the time they were

had a history of AID or not. In some studies, itwas not specified
if the patients still had the AID at the time they had their urticaria.
Some studies evaluated the prevalence ofmore than one AID. The selection
process of relevant studies is summarized in Table 1. In studies
identified, we did a search for the prevalence of autoantibodies
(antinuclear, rheumatoid factor, anti-dsDNA, anti-parietal cell, antitransglutaminase,

question: Was between-study variation (heterogeneity) minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers:
There is considerable heterogeneity in the design of the studies
we analyzed as well as in the patient demographics they included.
Many studies were small, some were uncontrolled for referral bias
and did not clearly differentiate CSU from acute urticaria, chronic inducible
urticaria and/or urticarial vasculitis. Furthermore, in many
studies the diagnostic criteria for AIDs were not described. The evaluation
of CSU prevalence in AIDs or vice versa was not the main aim
in many studies.

answers:

in combination with the terms that are listed in Table 1. No language

question: Did the interpretation of findings address all of the concerns identified in Domains 1 to 4?
answers:
There is considerable heterogeneity in the design of the studies
we analyzed as well as in the patient demographics they included.
Many studies were small, some were uncontrolled for referral bias
and did not clearly differentiate CSU from acute urticaria, chronic inducible
urticaria and/or urticarial vasculitis. Furthermore, in many
studies the diagnostic criteria for AIDs were not described. The evaluation
of CSU prevalence in AIDs or vice versa was not the main aim
in many studies. Therefore, the number of patients with CSU or AIDs
included in such studies may not be exhaustive. Some AIDs, for example
sarcoidosis, ulcerative colitis, Behcet's disease and Crohn's
disease, currently discussed as disorders with autoinflammatory features.
Our systematic review revealed a high variability in the prevalence
of AIDs in CSU between the studies. This imprecision is
likely to be due to design-specific issues or a variability in the baseline
patient characteristics. The study period (before 2017) is likely
to include studies of varying stringency of the diagnostic criteria for AIDs.
